meta.homeTitle: Japan Trip Planner — Free Shinkansen Seat Checker, Stays & Itineraries
meta.homeDesc: Plan your Japan trip with one tool. Find the Mt. Fuji seat on the Shinkansen, compare Tokyo stay areas, airport transfers, and a 7-day itinerary. Built by a Japanese creator.
meta.guideTitle: How to See Mt. Fuji from the Shinkansen — Seat, Side & Timing Guide
meta.guideDesc: Which seat to book for the best Mt. Fuji view on the Shinkansen? Seat E, right side (Tokyo→Osaka). Complete guide with season tips, car numbers, JR Pass vs ticket comparison, and FAQ.

home.brandTitle: Plan Your Japan Trip — From Mt. Fuji Seat to Stay
home.brandSubtitle: Free seat checker, itineraries, stay picks, and airport transfers — all in one place.
home.brandBuiltBy: Built by a Japanese creator for international travellers who don't want to miss the Mt. Fuji view.
home.brandSource: Based on publicly available information from JR Central
home.brandDisclaimer: This is an independent, unofficial tool and not endorsed by any JR company. Seat info is provided as-is; always follow official guidance at stations and on trains.
home.taglineBody: We tell you exactly which window seat to book for the Mt. Fuji view.
home.trainNote: Works for most Nozomi, Hikari and Kodama trains on the Tokaido Shinkansen line between Tokyo, Nagoya and Osaka/Kyoto.
home.readGuide: Read the full Mt. Fuji view guide
home.visUnavailable: Weather data unavailable – seat side is still correct, please check local forecast.
home.visLoadingFallback: Visibility forecast loading — refresh in a moment for live data.
home.visErrorFallback: Live visibility unavailable right now. Check season tips in the
home.visHigh: HIGH chance to see Mt. Fuji
home.resultSeatEMain: Seat E is the window seat on the Mt. Fuji side in most standard 3+2 cars.
home.resultSeatDFallback: If Seat E is sold out, Seat D is on the same side and still gives you a good chance to see Mt. Fuji.
home.resultVisError: Couldn't load weather data. Seat side is still correct, but please check the local forecast separately.
home.timingNote: Mt. Fuji is usually visible between Shin-Yokohama and Shizuoka, especially around Shin-Fuji station on clear days. You won't see it for the whole ride, so have your camera ready a bit before this section.
home.faqTitle: Quick FAQ about Mt. Fuji & seats

faq.a: Book seat D. It's on the same Mt. Fuji side, just one seat away from the window.
Layouts can be different, but the same left/right rule still applies for Mt. Fuji. Aim for a window seat on the Mt. Fuji side if possible.

faq.q: Is this seat good for photos?
faq.a: Yes. Window seat E gives you one of the best chances to take clear photos and videos of Mt. Fuji from the train, without other passengers directly in front of you.
No. Cloudy or hazy weather can hide it even from the correct seat.

tokyoBases.desc: Start with three common Tokyo bases: Shinjuku for energy, Ueno / Asakusa for easier east-side access, and Tokyo Station for Shinkansen and luggage convenience.

shinjuku.name: Shinjuku
shinjuku.bestFor: First-time energy, nightlife, shopping, and flexible train access.
shinjuku.weakness: Big stations and late-night crowds can feel intense on arrival day. Never follow street touts in Kabukicho — overcharging and scam bars are a real risk.

shibuya.name: Shibuya
shibuya.bestFor: Fashion, food, nightlife, youth culture, and easy west-side Tokyo access.
shibuya.weakness: Crowded, hilly around some hotels, and not as simple for early Shinkansen days.

ueno.name: Ueno
ueno.bestFor: Budget hotels, Narita access, museums, and market food.
ueno.weakness: Less late-night variety than Shinjuku or Shibuya.

asakusa.name: Asakusa
asakusa.bestFor: Traditional Tokyo, Senso-ji, riverside walks, and old-town texture.
asakusa.weakness: Not on the JR Yamanote Line, so cross-city trips need transfers.

tokyoStation.name: Tokyo Station
tokyoStation.bestFor: Early Shinkansen, clean logistics, Ginza access, and efficient travel days.
tokyoStation.weakness: More businesslike and usually pricier than Ueno or Asakusa.

eastTokyo.name: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa / East Tokyo
eastTokyo.bestFor: Quiet local Tokyo, coffee, gardens, museums, and slower mornings.
eastTokyo.weakness: Better as a calm detour or second-time base than a default first-night choice.

uenoAsakusa.name: Ueno / Asakusa
uenoAsakusa.bestFor: Narita access, budget stays, and old-town Tokyo
uenoAsakusa.weakness: less nightlife than Shinjuku or Shibuya

localTokyo.eyebrow: Local Tokyo picks from a Japanese local
localTokyo.desc: A Local Lens for quieter east-side neighborhoods beyond Shinjuku and Shibuya, including Oshiage for Skytree views and riverside walks.

kiyosumi.name: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa
kiyosumi.summary: Coffee, Kiyosumi Garden, Fukagawa Edo Museum, art museums, and slow canal-side walks. Best when you want a calm east-side morning rather than another busy sightseeing stop.
kiyosumi.avoidIf: you want nightlife or first-night simplicity

kuramae.name: Kuramae
kuramae.summary: Design shops, small makers, riverside cafes, and a softer Asakusa pairing.
kuramae.avoidIf: you need major sights in one stop

oshiage.name: Oshiage
oshiage.summary: Skytree views, Sumida riverside walks, and calmer east-side stays. Next-door Kinshicho has a spacious Daiso, Muji, and gashapon floor without Shibuya crowds.
oshiage.avoidIf: you want nightlife or a central feel

monzenNakacho.name: Monzen-Nakacho
monzenNakacho.summary: Old Tokyo streets, shrines, neighborhood izakaya, and easy East Tokyo add-ons.

ryogoku.name: Ryogoku
ryogoku.summary: Sumo culture, Edo history, museums, and a useful bridge toward Asakusa.

suitengumae.name: Suitengumae / Ningyocho
suitengumae.summary: T-CAT airport bus access, quiet hotels, Ningyocho food streets, and an easy bridge between Tokyo Station and East Tokyo.
suitengumae.bestFor: airport transfer, calm hotels, old-town food
suitengumae.avoidIf: you want nightlife or headline sights

guide.h1: How to see Mt. Fuji from the Shinkansen (Tokyo ⇄ Osaka/Kyoto)
guide.subtitle: A practical guide to the best Shinkansen seats, sections, and timing for Mt. Fuji views.
guide.writtenBy: Written by a Japanese local, for visitors who don't want to miss the Mt. Fuji × Shinkansen view.
guide.introH2: Mt. Fuji view basics on the Tokaido Shinkansen
guide.introP1: Many visitors to Japan want to see Mt. Fuji from the Shinkansen, but it's easy to miss if you don't know which side or section to sit in. This guide explains where and when Mt. Fuji is visible from the Tokaido Shinkansen between Tokyo and Osaka/Kyoto, and how to make it easier by using our free Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker tool.
guide.introP1Bold: where and when Mt. Fuji is visible from the Tokaido Shinkansen
guide.introP2: You won't see Mt. Fuji for the whole ride – it appears only for a short stretch of the journey, and it can be completely hidden by clouds on some days.

tldr.text: Tokyo → Osaka/Kyoto = right side. Osaka/Kyoto → Tokyo = left side.
In most standard 3+2 cars, Seat E is the Mt. Fuji window seat.
Around Shin-Fuji station, between Shin-Yokohama and Shizuoka.
Clear days, usually late morning to early afternoon (season-dependent).
Use the free Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker on fujiseat.com before you book.

guide.s1H2: Which side of the Shinkansen is Mt. Fuji on? (Seat E explained)
guide.s1Bullet1: Mt. Fuji is on the right side of the train (window seat E in most standard cars).
guide.s1Bullet2: Mt. Fuji is on the left side of the train (again, window seat E in most standard cars).
guide.s1Note: In most standard (non–Green Car) 3+2 seat cars, Seat E is the window seat on the Mt. Fuji side. Green Cars may have a different layout, but the same left/right rule still applies. Some older guides still mention different seat letters, but on most modern 3+2 standard cars, Seat E is the Mt. Fuji window seat.
guide.s2H2: Best section and timing to see Mt. Fuji from the train
guide.s2P1: You won't see Mt. Fuji for the whole ride – it appears only for a short stretch of the journey. Mt. Fuji is usually visible on clear days between Shin-Yokohama and Shizuoka, especially around Shin-Fuji station.
guide.s2Bullet1: Tokaido Shinkansen between Tokyo, Nagoya and Osaka/Kyoto (Nozomi / Hikari / Kodama).
guide.s2Bullet2: Late morning to early afternoon often gives a clearer silhouette. In summer, early morning can also work well.
guide.s2Bullet3: On cloudy or hazy days, Mt. Fuji may be hard to see even from the correct side and seat.
guide.s3H2: Practical seat tips for a better Mt. Fuji view
guide.s3Bullet1: When booking, choose a reserved seat in a standard car and request seat E on the Mt. Fuji side if possible.
guide.s3Bullet2: Window seats near the middle of the car often have the clearest views, away from doors and bulkheads.
guide.s3Bullet3: Mt. Fuji can appear and disappear quickly, especially around Shin-Fuji station.
guide.s3Bullet4: Some trains announce when Mt. Fuji is visible—listen for English announcements if you're not watching the map.
guide.s4H2: Common mistakes when trying to see Mt. Fuji
guide.s4Bullet1: Forgetting that Tokyo → Osaka/Kyoto is right side, and the opposite for the return.
guide.s4Bullet2: Even with the correct seat, clouds or haze can fully cover the mountain.
guide.s4Bullet3: If you start looking only near Shizuoka, you might miss the best view around Shin-Fuji.
guide.jrH2: JR Pass vs Single Ticket: Which is cheaper?
guide.jrPassWorth: JR Pass is usually worth comparing only if you take multiple long Shinkansen rides (e.g. Tokyo → Kyoto → Hiroshima → Tokyo). For a simple Tokyo ↔ Osaka round trip, single tickets are often cheaper.
guide.jrNozomiNote: Note: The Nozomi (fastest) may require an additional NOZOMI MIZUHO Ticket (approx. ¥4,960 Tokyo→Kyoto/Osaka) on top of your JR Pass. Without it, use Hikari or Kodama — these stop near Shin-Fuji, which is actually better for Mt. Fuji views anyway.
guide.seasonH2: Best Season & Time of Day to See Mt. Fuji

seasons.note: Clear skies, early snow from October

timeItems.text: Sun glare possible on the Mt. Fuji side

guide.proTip: Pro tip: Check Shizuoka Prefecture weather the night before. Our tool shows live visibility near Shin-Fuji at the top of the page.
carStandard: Cars 4–7 often give the clearest unobstructed views
Seat E = Mt. Fuji window seat in all standard cars
carGreen: Mt. Fuji window seat = Seat D

carRequest.text: select column E on the seat map
reserve free at any JR station counter

faq.q: Can I see Mt. Fuji from a non-reserved car?
faq.a: Yes, but risky — you may end up in an aisle seat with no view. Reserve Seat E in advance.

faq.q: How long can I see Mt. Fuji from the train?
faq.a: Only about 30–60 seconds at Shinkansen speed. Have your camera ready before reaching Shin-Fuji station.

faq.q: Is Seat E always the Mt. Fuji side?
faq.a: In standard 3+2 cars, yes. In Green Cars (2+2 layout), the Mt. Fuji window seat is Seat D.
Mt. Fuji is often hidden, especially in summer. Check our live visibility indicator at the top of the page.

faq.q: Can I see Mt. Fuji on the return trip (Osaka/Kyoto → Tokyo)?
faq.a: Yes — Mt. Fuji is on the LEFT side, which is again Seat E. Use our checker and select the opposite direction.

faq.q: Does the Nozomi stop near Mt. Fuji?
faq.a: No, but you can still see it through the window as the train passes. Hikari and Kodama stop at Shin-Fuji station.

faq.q: Is the JR Pass worth it for Tokyo–Osaka only?
faq.a: Generally no. Round trip ≈ ¥29,000 vs 7-day Pass ¥50,000. The Pass makes sense if also visiting Hiroshima, Nara, etc.

faq.q: Can I bring large luggage on the Shinkansen?
faq.a: Bags with total dimensions over 160cm and up to 250cm require a seat reservation with an oversized baggage area (予約が必要). Reserve this when booking your Shinkansen seat. Bags over 250cm are not permitted.

faq.q: Is there WiFi on the Shinkansen?
faq.a: Onboard WiFi exists but can be unreliable. A Japan eSIM is recommended for consistent connectivity throughout your trip.

faq.q: What is the best way to book as a foreigner?
faq.a: Klook — fully in English, instant mobile voucher, and you can select Seat E on the seat map.

guide.makeEasyH2: Make it easy with the Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker
guide.makeEasyP1: If you don't want to remember all the details, you can use our free Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker. Just choose your direction and it tells you which seat to book for the best chance of seeing Mt. Fuji.
guide.makeEasyP2: The tool is designed to work well on mobile, so you can quickly check it while booking tickets or travelling in Japan.
guide.commandCenterP: Got your Shinkansen seat sorted? Plan the rest of the trip with route templates, booking essentials, weather, currency and a pre-departure checklist.
guide.priorityH2: Priority Seats — What Foreigners Should Know
guide.priorityP1: You'll notice seats marked 優先席 (yūsen-seki) near the doors of most Japanese trains. These are priority seats reserved for elderly passengers, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those carrying small children.
guide.priorityP2: While not legally prohibited, sitting in priority seats when you don't need them is considered poor manners in Japan. Many Japanese people avoid these seats entirely — even when the train is not crowded — because occupying them carries an unspoken social stigma.
guide.priorityP3: As a visitor, the safest approach is to simply avoid priority seats altogether. If the train is packed and no one who needs the seat is nearby, sitting briefly is understandable — but be ready to stand immediately when someone who needs it boards.
guide.priorityTip: Look for the stickers on windows and the different-colored seat fabric (usually grey or patterned) to identify priority seats. When in doubt, choose a regular seat.
guide.devP: I'm also building a small side project called KanjiFlick — a swipe-style list of single kanji meanings (the kind Japanese kids learn). It's not a travel app, but if you're curious about the characters you'll see around Japan, you might enjoy it.

feedback.title: Share a review, kind note, or Japan travel tip
feedback.description: Send an anonymous note about what was useful, confusing, missing, or worth covering next. Good experiences, small reviews, and local tips are just as helpful as bug reports.
feedback.playful: If fujiseat helped, a tiny compliment also makes the Japanese creator very happy.
feedback.placeholder: Tell me what was useful, confusing, missing, or worth covering. Example: I used this before Tokyo to Kyoto and the seat tip helped, but the booking step was confusing.
feedback.note: This is anonymous and mainly for improving fujiseat and finding future article ideas. Please do not send passport numbers, booking references, payment details, or other sensitive information.
feedback.sent: Thank you. Your feedback has been sent.
feedback.error: Could not send your feedback. Please try again later.
feedback.privacyNote: Feedback is collected anonymously for site improvement and possible future article ideas. I do not ask for contact details, so there is no reply workflow.

kiyosumi-shirakawa.title: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa Guide — Coffee, Gardens and Quiet East Tokyo
kiyosumi-shirakawa.description: A practical local guide to Kiyosumi-Shirakawa, Tokyo's quiet coffee, garden and art neighborhood. Best for slow mornings, museums, and travelers who want a calmer side of Tokyo.
kiyosumi-shirakawa.combineWith: Monzen-Nakacho, Ryogoku, Asakusa, or Tokyo Station.
kiyosumi-shirakawa.whyVisit: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa is useful when Tokyo starts to feel too loud. It gives travelers a grounded east-side morning: independent coffee, a classic garden, compact museums, and streets that still feel residential.
route: Coffee near Kiyosumi-Shirakawa Station
Kiyosumi Garden
Fukagawa Edo Museum
Museum of Contemporary Art Tokyo or a river walk
Continue to Monzen-Nakacho or Ryogoku

developerPicks.title: Developer's local coffee picks
developerPicks.description: Two small coffee stops I would personally point visitors to when they want the quieter, more local side of Kiyosumi-Shirakawa.

items.name: COFFEE STAND OZ
items.description: A small coffee stand tucked quietly in a back alley, useful when you want a local stop that does not feel like a tourist checklist.

items.name: ROAM COFFEE
items.description: Open every Sunday. The coffee concept is sweetness, clarity, and a bright acidity that leaves you feeling positive. ROAM COFFEE pursues a taste that makes the drinker's everyday life feel a little lighter and more forward-looking, and you can feel the care behind it.

kiyosumi-shirakawa.shouldStay: Stay here if you have already solved the basics and want a quiet local base. For a first night in Tokyo, Shinjuku, Ueno, Asakusa, or Tokyo Station are usually simpler. Kiyosumi-Shirakawa is strongest as a quiet detour, second-time base, or calm final night before a Tokyo Station departure.

kuramae.title: Kuramae Guide — Design Shops, Cafes and Makers in East Tokyo
kuramae.description: A practical local guide to Kuramae, Tokyo's quiet design and maker neighborhood. Best for craft shops, riverside cafes, and travelers who want a creative, walkable side of east Tokyo.
kuramae.whyVisit: Kuramae is where Tokyo's design and craft scene lives without the tourist markup. Old warehouses turned into leather studios, glass workshops, and single-origin roasters line quiet streets between the Sumida River and Asakusa. It rewards slow browsing: walk into a shop, talk to the maker, and buy something you cannot find in Shibuya.
route: Coffee at a Kuramae roaster near the station
Browse design shops along Kuramae-dori and side streets
Walk along the Sumida River toward Asakusa
Lunch at a local cafe or continue to Asakusa for traditional food
Optional: add Kiyosumi-Shirakawa for gardens and museums
kuramae.shouldStay: Good for repeat visitors or travelers who value design over sightseeing density. First-timers usually do better near Asakusa or Ueno. Kuramae is one station away and easy as a morning add-on without sleeping there. If you want a creative, quiet base and do not mind being a short ride from major sights, it works well.

monzen-nakacho.title: Monzen-Nakacho Guide — Shrines, Izakaya and Old East Tokyo
monzen-nakacho.description: A practical local guide to Monzen-Nakacho, Tokyo's old-town neighborhood with shrines, izakaya streets, and local food. Best for afternoon walks, traditional atmosphere, and travelers who want a slower east-side evening.
monzen-nakacho.whyVisit: Monzen-Nakacho is one of the few Tokyo neighborhoods where the old shitamachi atmosphere still holds. Tomioka Hachimangu shrine anchors the area, and the surrounding streets are lined with small izakaya, local lunch spots, and traditional shops that cater to regulars, not tourists. It gives travelers a taste of Tokyo's east-side character without the curated feel of Yanaka or Asakusa.
route: Start from Kiyosumi-Shirakawa or arrive at Monzen-Nakacho Station
Visit Tomioka Hachimangu shrine
Walk the backstreets for local shops and small eateries
Early evening izakaya along Eitai-dori or side alleys
Optional: continue to Tsukiji or Tokyo Station for dinner
monzen-nakacho.shouldStay: Monzen-Nakacho works as a local base for travelers who already know their way around Tokyo and want a quieter, more residential feel. For first-timers, it is better as a half-day add-on paired with Kiyosumi-Shirakawa in the morning. The Tozai line gives easy access to Nihonbashi and Otemachi for business travelers.

oshiage.title: Oshiage Guide — Skytree, Local Streets and a Quieter Asakusa Alternative
oshiage.description: A practical local guide to Oshiage, the Tokyo Skytree neighborhood. Best for Skytree views, Solamachi shopping, and travelers who want a calmer alternative to Asakusa with easy airport access.
oshiage.bestTiming: Late afternoon into evening for Skytree lights. Morning for quiet streets.
oshiage.whyVisit: Oshiage gives travelers the Tokyo Skytree experience plus a surprisingly quiet residential neighborhood one block away. The Solamachi mall at the base of Skytree covers souvenirs, food, and a planetarium. Beyond the tower, Oshiage's backstreets feel like old shitamachi Tokyo: small shops, local eateries, and none of the Asakusa crowds. The Tobu Skytree line and Hanzomon metro make Narita and Haneda access straightforward.

extra.title: Kinshicho — the next station over
extra.body: One stop from Oshiage on the Hanzomon line, Kinshicho has no headline sights but is quietly useful for shopping. The large commercial complex next to the station houses a spacious Daiso, Muji, and a big gashapon capsule toy floor, all far less crowded than their Shibuya or Shinjuku equivalents. Don Quijote is also nearby.

route: Arrive at Oshiage Station and walk to Tokyo Skytree
Browse Solamachi for souvenirs or lunch
Walk the quiet backstreets east of the tower
Cross toward Asakusa along the Sumida River
Optional: one stop to Kinshicho for Daiso, Muji, and gashapon shopping
Optional: Skytree observation deck in the late afternoon for sunset views
oshiage.shouldStay: Oshiage is a practical budget alternative to Asakusa. Hotels are cheaper, airport access is direct via Tobu Skytree line and Keisei line, and Asakusa is a 20-minute walk or one stop away. The trade-off: fewer restaurants and less evening atmosphere than Asakusa. It works well for a first or last night when airport logistics matter, or as a budget base for travelers who do not mind a quieter neighborhood.

ryogoku.title: Ryogoku Guide — Sumo, Edo History and Museums in East Tokyo
ryogoku.description: A practical local guide to Ryogoku, Tokyo's sumo and Edo history district. Best for the Edo-Tokyo Museum, sumo culture, and travelers who want a historical side of east Tokyo.
ryogoku.whyVisit: Ryogoku is where Tokyo's sumo tradition and Edo-period history live side by side. The Kokugikan sumo arena hosts major tournaments three times a year, and even outside tournament season the neighborhood is full of sumo stables, chanko nabe restaurants, and the Edo-Tokyo Museum. It is a focused, half-day destination that pairs naturally with Asakusa across the river.
route: Arrive at Ryogoku Station on the JR Sobu line
Edo-Tokyo Museum for a thorough Edo and Meiji overview
Walk past Kokugikan and look for sumo wrestlers near stables
Chanko nabe lunch at a local restaurant
Cross the Sumida River to Asakusa or continue to Kiyosumi-Shirakawa
ryogoku.shouldStay: Ryogoku is best as a half-day visit rather than a base. Hotel options are limited compared to Asakusa or Ueno, and evening dining is quieter than most travelers prefer. Visit for the museum, sumo, and chanko, then continue to a busier area. If you want to stay nearby, Asakusa is across the river and offers more options.

suitengumae-ningyocho.title: Suitengumae / Ningyocho Guide — T-CAT, Hotels and Old Tokyo Food Streets
suitengumae-ningyocho.description: A practical local guide to Suitengumae and Ningyocho, Tokyo's airport bus hub with calm hotels, old-town food streets and easy access to Nihonbashi, Tokyo Station and East Tokyo.
suitengumae-ningyocho.bestTiming: Arrival day, departure eve, or a light half-day.
suitengumae-ningyocho.combineWith: Ningyocho, Nihonbashi, Kiyosumi-Shirakawa, or Tokyo Station.
suitengumae-ningyocho.whyVisit: Suitengumae is not a flashy sightseeing stop, and that is exactly why it is useful. The Tokyo City Air Terminal makes airport bus transfers simple, hotels are calmer than Shinjuku or Shibuya, and Ningyocho is close enough for old-town snacks, small restaurants, and a more local first evening. It works as a practical East Tokyo hub rather than a destination you force into the itinerary.
route: Arrive via T-CAT or Suitengumae Station
Walk toward Ningyocho for old-town food streets
Stop by Amazake Yokocho or small local snack shops
Continue to Nihonbashi or Tokyo Station if you need city logistics
Optional: add Kiyosumi-Shirakawa for coffee, gardens, and museums
suitengumae-ningyocho.shouldStay: Stay here if airport transfer, quiet hotels, and easy movement matter more than nightlife. It is especially useful for a first night after a long flight, a final night before an airport bus, or travelers who want to be near Tokyo Station without paying Tokyo Station prices. If you want big sights at your door, Asakusa, Ueno, or Shinjuku will feel clearer.

localTokyoIndex.title: Local Tokyo Picks — Quiet Neighborhoods Beyond Shinjuku and Shibuya
localTokyoIndex.description: Explore quieter Tokyo neighborhoods like Kiyosumi-Shirakawa, Kuramae, Monzen-Nakacho and Ryogoku, chosen by a Japanese local for coffee, gardens, museums and slow walks.
localTokyoIndex.quickTitle: Use Local Tokyo as a calm second layer.
localTokyoIndex.quickBody: First-time visitors usually do better sleeping in Shinjuku, Ueno, Asakusa, or Tokyo Station. Add East Tokyo when you want coffee, gardens, museums, and slower streets without turning the trip into a hotel search problem.
localTokyoIndex.routeBody: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa in the morning, then continue to Monzen-Nakacho or Ryogoku. It works well before Asakusa, Tokyo Station, or an easy dinner plan.
halfDayStops: Walk Kiyosumi Garden when you want a quiet reset
Visit Fukagawa Edo Museum for old Tokyo context
Continue to Monzen-Nakacho for shrines or Ryogoku for Edo and sumo history
